serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of honey is approximately 1 teaspoon (7 g), commonly used as a sweetener in beverages, on toast, or as a condiment.

nutrient_source.title: Honey — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for honey (CR ID F13030001). All core macros and secondary nutrients confirmed from this source.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value 58 — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Honey has a well-established GI of approximately 58 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (58 × 82.1 × 7g serving) / (100 × 100) = approximately 4.1 g per 7 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit honey intake, as its concentrated sugar load (82.1 g/100 g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and contributes to elevated blood pressure; use only in trace amounts as an occasional flavour enhancer and do not treat it as a heart-safe alternative to table sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; GI of 58 still causes significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid honey as a sweetener because its 82.1 g sugar per 100 g causes significant blood glucose spikes regardless of its medium GI; non-caloric alternatives such as stevia are preferred, and even small amounts contribute meaningfully to daily carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) with negligible fat and zero sodium; excess sugar intake is linked to dyslipidaemia, elevated triglycerides, and increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should use honey sparingly, as its very high free-sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) can raise serum triglycerides and contribute to dyslipidaemia; while honey contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, the sugar load outweighs any trace antioxidant benefit at typical consumption levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar content of 82.1 g/100 g far exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory mediators TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular honey consumption because its 82.1 g sugar per 100 g promotes systemic inflammation via TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways, and trace polyphenols in raw honey are insufficient to offset this pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey is a naturally occurring sweet syrup produced by honeybees (Apis mellifera and related species) from the nectar of flowers. It is one of the oldest sweeteners used by humans and is consumed globally across diverse culinary traditions. Honey is minimally processed — it is typically extracted from honeycombs by centrifugation and may be lightly filtered or pasteurised, but its core composition remains close to its natural state. Its flavour profile ranges from delicately floral to richly caramel-like depending on the botanical source of the nectar (e.g., acacia, manuka, clover, tualang). The texture is viscous and syrupy at room temperature and may crystallise over time, which is a natural physical change and does not indicate spoilage. Nutritionally, honey is almost entirely composed of carbohydrates — approximately 82.1 g per 100 g — predominantly as fructose and glucose, with negligible protein (0.2 g/100 g) and zero fat. Its energy density is 315 kcal per 100 g. Honey has a glycemic index of approximately 58 (medium), which is slightly lower than table sugar (GI ~65), but its extremely high sugar concentration means that even small servings deliver a significant glycaemic load. Clinically, honey is not a safe substitute for sugar in diabetes management, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease contexts. It is not suitable for infants under 12 months due to the risk of Clostridium botulinum spores. Honey is gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher, but is not vegan. It is commonly used as a sweetener in beverages, baked goods, marinades, dressings, and traditional remedies.
common_user_queries: Can I eat honey if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of honey?
How much sugar is in honey?
Is raw honey healthier than regular honey?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F13030001, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. GI value of 58 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database for honey. All core macros and secondary nutrients are confirmed from the primary source.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero — honey is a pure carbohydrate-sugar product containing no fat by its natural composition, confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo database. saturated_fat_g is naturally zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — honey contains no dietary fibre, confirmed per HPB. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero — honey is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for honey are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 7 g (1 teaspoon): (58 × 82.1 / 100) × (7 / 100) × 100 = approximately 4.1 g GL per serving. Honey is not suitable for infants under 12 months due to risk of Clostridium botulinum spores. This record is a generic food entry (not cuisine-specific), so no mandatory multilingual local-language names apply under G6 rules.
